questions.question_text: Under Maryland's Statute of Frauds, which element is required for a real estate contract to be enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: Written and signed by the parties
questions.explanation: Maryland's Statute of Frauds requires real estate contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties to be enforceable. Notarization, filing, and witnessing are not required for contract enforceability.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an offer on a Maryland home contingent on financing, inspection, and appraisal. The seller accepts but the appraisal comes in $20,000 below the purchase price. What is the buyer's most likely option under the appraisal contingency?
distractors: Sue the appraiser for the difference
Demand the seller lower the price by law
questions.explanation: An appraisal contingency protects the buyer if the property appraises below the contract price. The buyer can renegotiate the price with the seller or withdraw from the contract without forfeiting the deposit.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Maryland REALTORS® standard contract forms is accurate?
questions.correct_answer: They are available but not legally mandated
distractors: They must be used for all residential sales
They are required by state licensing law
questions.explanation: Maryland REALTORS® (formerly the Maryland Association of REALTORS) publishes standard contract forms that are widely used, but their use is not legally mandated. Parties may use other forms as long as essential contract elements are present.

questions.question_text: A Maryland contract of sale contains the parties, price, and signatures but no description of the property at all. Is it enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: No, an adequate property description is essential
distractors: Yes, if both parties agree orally on which property is meant
Yes, if an earnest money deposit has been paid
questions.explanation: A contract of sale must describe the property well enough to identify it with reasonable certainty. A formal legal description is not required — a street address that identifies the property usually suffices — but a contract with no description at all cannot be enforced. The deed, by contrast, carries a full legal description.

questions.question_text: A buyer's offer includes a contingency for the sale of the buyer's existing home. The seller accepts but later receives a higher offer. Which outcome is most accurate under a typical Maryland contract with a kick-out clause?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer gets notice to remove the contingency or lose the contract
distractors: Seller may accept the new offer immediately
questions.explanation: A kick-out clause allows the seller to continue marketing the home. If a better offer arrives, the original buyer typically receives notice (often 72 hours) to waive the home-sale contingency or release the seller from the contract.

questions.question_text: Under Maryland law, who may prepare a deed so that it can be recorded?
questions.correct_answer: An attorney or a party named in the deed
questions.explanation: Under RP §3-104(f)(1), a deed may not be recorded unless it certifies that it was prepared by an attorney (or under an attorney's supervision) or by a party named in the instrument. Maryland is not an attorney-closing state — settlements are commonly conducted by licensed title insurance producers — but a real estate agent who is not a party may not prepare a deed, as that would be the unauthorized practice of law.

questions.question_text: A title company is conducting a Maryland residential settlement. Which task may the title company perform without an attorney?
distractors: Prepare the deed for the parties
Provide legal advice about title defects
Draft a power of attorney for the seller
questions.explanation: In Maryland, licensed title insurance producers may conduct real estate settlements — no attorney is required to close. However, a recorded deed must be prepared by an attorney or a party to it (RP §3-104(f)), and giving legal advice or drafting legal instruments for others would be the unauthorized practice of law.

questions.question_text: A buyer is purchasing a home with a mortgage in Maryland. Which types of title insurance are typically available?
distractors: Title insurance is not used in Maryland
questions.explanation: Maryland offers both owner's title insurance (protecting the buyer) and lender's title insurance (protecting the mortgage lender). The lender's policy is typically required, while the owner's policy is optional but recommended.

questions.question_text: During a title search on a Maryland property, an unrecorded easement is discovered. What should happen before settlement proceeds?
questions.correct_answer: The easement must be evaluated and disclosed
distractors: The seller must remove it immediately
The lender absorbs the risk automatically
questions.explanation: Title searches are standard in Maryland to identify encumbrances. An unrecorded easement must be evaluated to determine its impact, and the buyer must be informed so they can decide whether to proceed or negotiate.

questions.question_text: A Maryland buyer asks their real estate agent to prepare the deed for the transaction. How should the agent respond?
questions.correct_answer: Decline — a deed must be prepared by an attorney or a party to it
distractors: The agent can draft it with broker approval
Standard deed forms may be completed by anyone
The agent may draft it for a separate fee
questions.explanation: Under RP §3-104(f)(1), a recorded deed must certify that it was prepared by an attorney or by a party named in the instrument. A real estate licensee who is not a party may not draft deeds or other legal instruments — doing so is the unauthorized practice of law. (Deeds of trust are exempt from the certification rule and are customarily prepared by the lender.)

questions.question_text: In a typical Maryland residential transaction, which cost is the buyer's responsibility?
questions.explanation: Buyers in Maryland typically pay lender fees, prepaids (such as homeowner's insurance and property tax escrow), title insurance, and their share of transfer and recordation taxes. Commission and mortgage payoffs are seller costs.

questions.question_text: A seller reviews their settlement statement and sees a charge for outstanding property taxes. This charge represents:
questions.correct_answer: A lien that must be paid at closing
distractors: The buyer's share of future taxes
questions.explanation: Outstanding property tax liens on the property must be satisfied at closing. The seller is responsible for paying any unpaid property taxes from the sale proceeds, as shown on the settlement statement.

questions.question_text: Under Maryland law, if the contract of sale is silent, how are state and local transfer and recordation taxes divided between buyer and seller?
questions.correct_answer: Split equally between buyer and seller
questions.explanation: Under RP §14-104(b), unless otherwise negotiated in the contract, recordation taxes and state and local transfer taxes are shared equally between grantor and grantee. Exception: for a first-time Maryland homebuyer, the seller pays the entire state transfer tax (and, absent agreement, the buyer's usual half of the other taxes shifts as provided by §14-104(c)).

questions.question_text: A buyer's settlement statement shows recordation taxes, transfer taxes, title insurance, lender origination fees, and a broker commission charge. The buyer's representation agreement provides that the seller will pay all broker compensation. Should the buyer question the commission charge?
questions.correct_answer: Yes — the agreement puts the compensation on the seller
distractors: No, commission is split equally by law
Only if the buyer has no agent
questions.explanation: Listing commissions are customarily a seller cost under the listing agreement. Since 2024, buyers who agree in a written buyer-representation agreement to pay their broker may properly see a commission charge at closing — but this buyer's agreement assigns compensation to the seller, so the charge should be questioned and corrected.

questions.question_text: A settlement statement shows the buyer paying recordation tax on both the deed and the purchase-money deed of trust recorded with it. Is this correct in Maryland?
questions.correct_answer: No, a purchase-money deed of trust is exempt
distractors: Yes, recordation tax always applies to both instruments
No, the seller pays all recordation taxes
questions.explanation: Under Tax-Property §12-108(i), a purchase money mortgage or deed of trust is exempt from recordation tax if it recites on its face that it secures purchase money and is executed and recorded within 30 days of the deed. In a typical purchase, recordation tax is paid on the deed based on the consideration, but not again on the purchase-money deed of trust. Non-purchase-money financing (such as a later refinance above the old principal) is taxable.
